turns.content: I'm exploring Rust for a proof-of-concept in embedded firmware. I've read about attributes like #[link_name] and #[link_section], but I'm not quite sure how they work or when to use them.

Ah, good question! Those are some advanced features of Rust's `#[repr(C)]` and `#[link_name = "c_func_name"]` attributes. When you want your Rust code to interface with C libraries or embedded hardware, these can be very handy. For example, if you have a Rust ISR handler but need it to match the name expected by your C runtime, you'd use `#[link_name]`. It's like renaming a file in an operating system—just so that when the linker looks for symbols, it finds them under the right name.
Hey, I'm learning Rust. Can you explain how to link Rust functions with C symbols in embedded firmware?

Sure! When working on embedded firmware, sometimes you want your Rust-generated code to interoperate with existing C libraries or hardware drivers. One way to do this is by using the `#[link_name]` attribute. It allows you to specify a different name for exported functions when they're linked into the final executable.

I'm looking at this embedded module where my colleague used #[link_name = "c_func"] to match a C symbol with a different name, but I also saw #[link_section = ".data"] being applied elsewhere. Can you explain the difference between these two attributes?

Sure! The #[link_name] attribute is specifically for telling the linker how to map a Rust function or static item to a C symbol that has a different name, which is super useful when your C library uses a different convention than what you'd normally expect. On the other hand, #[link_section = ".data"] controls where in the binary file that item gets placed, like telling it to go into the .data section instead of .text or .bss. In embedded firmware for ISR tables, you might use link_name to match C interrupt handlers while using link_section to ensure those ISR entries land exactly where your linker script expects them. Are you working with a specific linker script that has custom sections defined?

Yeah, our linker script defines a special .isr_table section and we're trying to make sure all the interrupt vectors end up there without breaking the load order. So I'm wondering if #[link_section] affects how the Rust code interacts with std::sync::OnceLock or other runtime types?

That's a great catch, but #[link_section] only affects compile-time layout and static initialization; it doesn't change how the item behaves at runtime like std::sync::OnceLock does. The section attribute just tells the linker where to place the symbol in the final ELF or PE file, while types like OnceLock manage actual allocation and synchronization once the binary is loaded. For your ISR table, you'd typically use #[link_section = ".isr_table"] alongside #[link_name] to ensure each interrupt vector lands exactly where needed without interfering with normal heap management.
